turns.text: Hello Chasity, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly, careful not to wake Leo, and tucks a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for asking. Honestly, I... I'm not sure where to even start. It's only been a couple weeks, and everything feels kind of surreal.

I think the thing is—and maybe this sounds weird coming from someone who works in healthcare—but I felt kind of invisible during the whole process? Like, because I'm young and my pregnancy was straightforward and I work as a nurse, everyone just assumed I had it all figured out. My OB was great, don't get me wrong, but after delivery, it was like a light switch. Suddenly I was just... handed my baby and sent home.

*laughs softly, a bit shakily*

The funny part is, knowing what I know professionally made everything harder, not easier. I was waiting for those newborn screening results like my life depended on it. I *know* what those conditions look like. I've explained them to terrified parents. And then I'm standing there as the terrified parent, and nobody really talked me through what to expect emotionally.

And being unmarried, in a small town where I literally work with half these people? That's its own thing. Everyone's congratulating me and saying I'm doing great, but they're also... I don't know. Noticing. Wondering.

*looks down at Leo*

The hardest part is I can't seem to just *be* his mom without analyzing everything. Does that make sense?
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

Oh man. Okay, so physically? I was not prepared for that. I mean, I *knew* intellectually what postpartum recovery looks like—I've explained it to patients—but experiencing it is... it's humbling. The bleeding, the soreness, just feeling like my body wasn't mine anymore. And nobody really prepares you for how *tired* you are. Not just tired from the baby—though Leo kept me up those first nights—but bone-deep exhausted from the actual trauma of labor.

*pauses, fidgeting with the blanket*

Emotionally though... that's where I really struggled. The first few days in the hospital, I was running on adrenaline and pain medication. I felt this rush of love looking at Leo, but also this terror I couldn't name. Like, "Oh God, what have I done? Can I actually do this?" And nobody asked me about that part. They just kept checking my vitals and asking if I was eating enough.

When I got home, the weight of it hit different. I was alone with this tiny human, and suddenly all those clinical things I know became... personal? I was obsessing over his color, his breathing, checking him constantly. My mom came to help for a week, and I think she noticed I was wound way too tight, but we didn't really talk about it.

*voice gets quieter*

I also felt really alone in being unmarried. Like I was the cautionary tale everyone was silently judging. That isolation made the emotional stuff feel even heavier.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

*shifts uncomfortably, glancing down at Leo*

Yeah, okay. So I knew I wanted to breastfeed. That was the plan from the beginning—I'd seen the benefits, read all the research. Very clinical decision-making on my part, right? But then... reality happened.

*rubs eyes*

The hospital lactation consultant came in, and she was helpful enough, but I felt like she was rushing. She got me positioned, showed me the latch, and then she left. And I'm sitting there thinking, "Wait, is this right? Does it feel the way it's supposed to?" I have the knowledge in my head, but I couldn't apply it to my own body. It was actually *painful* those first few days—like, I knew that was normal, that it would pass, but knowing didn't make it hurt less.

*voice gets a bit frustrated*

What really got me was that I felt like I couldn't ask for help without admitting I didn't know what I was doing. I'm a nurse. People expect me to just... know. So I sat there in pain, Googling latch positions at 3 AM like I wasn't a healthcare professional.

And then there's the mental load of it. Every time Leo cries, I'm analyzing—is he hungry? Wet? Gassy? My clinical brain is exhausting my mom brain. I'll admit I've had moments where I've wanted to just give him formula and breathe, but then I feel guilty. Like I'm supposed to be strong enough to do this.

*looks up*

It's gotten better, but those first two weeks? Brutal.
How did you decide how to feed your baby — and did you feel that decision was truly yours to make?

*lets out a long breath, looking a bit conflicted*

That's... that's actually a really good question. Because when I say I "decided" to breastfeed, I'm not sure how much of that was actually my choice versus what I absorbed from working in healthcare and living in this environment.

*picks at a thread on the blanket*

In my training, breastfeeding is presented as the gold standard. It's *the* right choice. And I internalized that—hard. So when I got pregnant, it wasn't really a decision I sat down and made thoughtfully. It was just... assumed. Of course I'd breastfeed. I'm educated, I have access, I'm young and healthy. It's what you're supposed to do.

But did anyone actually ask me what *I* wanted? What would work for *my* life? Not really. My OB mentioned it in passing like it was a foregone conclusion. My family expected it. My colleagues definitely expected it.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

If I'm being real, I don't know if I would've chosen it if I'd felt like I actually *could* choose something else without judgment. Like, formula-feeding would've made those early weeks so much easier for me mentally. But admitting that felt impossible. I'm a pediatric nurse. People would've thought I didn't care, or that I was lazy.

*looks at Leo*

I'm making it work now, and I do love the bonding part once it stopped hurting. But I wish someone had sat down with me and said, "What matters most to *you*, Chasity?" instead of assuming they already knew.
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

*eyes get glassy, and I take a moment before speaking*

Okay. The full picture. Yeah.

*voice shaky*

I've been really struggling. Like, more than I've admitted to anyone except my mom, and even then I downplay it. There's this constant anxiety that won't shut off. Every sound Leo makes, I'm analyzing it. Every sigh, every slight color change—my brain immediately goes to worst-case scenarios. Jaundice, sepsis, metabolic crisis. I *know* the odds are in our favor, but knowing doesn't stop the spiral.

And then there's the guilt on top of it. Because I'm supposed to be enjoying this, right? I'm supposed to feel like a natural, like this is the most beautiful time of my life. Instead, I'm exhausted and paranoid and honestly? Some days I resent how much my body and my time aren't mine anymore.

*wipes eyes quickly*

The isolation is real too. I see my coworkers and they treat me differently now. Like I'm suddenly less of a professional and more of a cautionary tale—the young, unmarried nurse with a baby. I can feel it in how they interact with me. And I can't exactly go out and be myself because I'm tethered to this feeding schedule and Leo's needs.

*voice cracks slightly*

There are moments—and I hate saying this out loud—where I wonder if I made a mistake. Not with Leo, never with Leo. But with the timing, with my life. I'm twenty, and everyone's moving forward while I'm stuck in this postpartum fog, feeling like I'm failing at both motherhood and my career.

I haven't talked to my doctor about any of this.
Were there feelings that surprised you or were harder to admit — to yourself or to others?

*looks away, jaw tightening*

Yeah. There definitely were.

*long pause*

The hardest one to admit, even to myself, is... resentment. Toward Leo sometimes. Not him specifically—he's perfect, he didn't ask for any of this. But I resent what his existence has done to my life. I had plans. Real plans. I was building a career in neonatal screening, and I was good at it. And now I'm trying to figure out how to even keep working full-time while taking care of him, and it's like... nobody talks about that trade-off. They just expect you to figure it out.

*voice gets smaller*

And there's this ugly feeling I have sometimes where I'm jealous of my friends who don't have kids. Like, genuinely envious watching them go out on a Friday night while I'm home smelling like spit-up and leaking from places I didn't know could leak. That makes me sound like a terrible mother, doesn't it?

*quickly adds*

I love Leo. I *do*. But I also grieve the version of myself that existed two months ago. The one who was just a nurse, just a person, not constantly defined by being a mom.

*fidgets with her hands*

And honestly? There's this shame underneath it all. Like I should be grateful, like I should just be happy and glowing. The fact that I'm not means something's wrong with me. So I hide it. I smile at work, I talk about how natural I am, and I go home and cry in the shower where Leo can't hear me.

*looks up with wet eyes*

I think I might need help.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

*shifts position carefully, wincing slightly*

The physical stuff... honestly, it's been slower than I expected, even knowing what I know. Like, I understood the timeline intellectually, but actually living through it is different.

*rubs lower back*

The first week was rough. I was bleeding heavily, sore everywhere—perineal pain, cramping, my breasts felt like they were going to explode. I had a small tear that needed a couple stitches, nothing major, but sitting down was just... ugh. And nobody really prepared me for how long the bleeding lasts. I kept thinking it should be done by now, and then I'd soak through another pad.

The sleep deprivation made everything worse. I couldn't recover properly because I was up all night, and then my body couldn't heal as fast. It's a vicious cycle.

*pauses*

As for support... my mom was here for a week, which helped. She made sure I ate and tried to get me to rest while she watched Leo. But after she left, I was kind of on my own. My OB's office said everything looked good at my six-week checkup, and that was it. No follow-up, no "how are you actually doing?" Just cleared to exercise and resume normal activities.

*laughs bitterly*

Normal activities. I'm still having pelvic pain, my incision area still feels weird sometimes, and I'm terrified to even try working out. But there's this expectation that at six weeks, you're magically healed and ready to bounce back.

*looks down*

I didn't ask for more help because... I don't know. Pride? Not wanting to be a burden?
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes distant*

Oh God. Everything changed. Like, fundamentally.

*voice gets quieter*

I used to be... I don't know, just Chasity? The competent nurse who knew her stuff, who people respected at work. I had routines—I'd go to the gym, meet friends for coffee, read medical journals in my downtime. I had a *self* that existed outside of caregiving. And now? I don't even recognize my days anymore.

My mornings used to start with coffee and planning. Now they start at whatever hour Leo decides to wake up, and I'm instantly in survival mode. By the time I think about showering, it's afternoon. I can't remember the last time I finished a sentence without being interrupted.

*fidgets with the blanket*

And my relationships... they've fractured in ways I didn't expect. My friends without kids don't really get it. They invite me out and seem confused when I say I can't just leave Leo for hours. Like, I'm suddenly boring or unavailable. We're drifting, and that hurts.

*voice wavers*

With my colleagues, it's complicated. They see me differently now. I'm not just the smart young nurse anymore—I'm the cautionary tale, the girl who had a baby. Some of them are supportive, but others? I catch the looks. The judgment.

*looks down at Leo, then back up*

And I've lost... me. I don't know who I am right now. I'm Leo's mom, which is huge and important, but it's like that consumed everything else I was. I look in the mirror some days and don't recognize myself—not just physically, but like, spiritually? I'm empty. And I feel guilty for even thinking that because I *wanted* this baby.
Were there parts of your previous self — your work identity, social life, sense of independence — that felt hard to hold onto?

*nods slowly, eyes getting wet*

Yeah. Really hard.

*voice breaks slightly*

My work identity was... it was everything, you know? I spent years training to be good at what I do. I genuinely care about those newborn screenings, about catching things early. And I was building something—like, I had goals, momentum. I was getting recognition, being asked to mentor newer staff. That felt *good*. It felt like I was becoming someone.

And now when I go back—because I'm trying to hold onto some shifts, trying not to completely disappear from my career—I feel like I'm phoning it in. I'm exhausted, distracted, worried about Leo. My colleagues can probably tell I'm not all there. It's like I'm slowly becoming irrelevant at the one place where I felt competent and valued.

*wipes eyes*

The social life thing is brutal too. I had this friend group, nothing crazy, but we'd do stuff together. And I've basically ghosted them without meaning to. It's not that I don't want to see them—I *do*—but the logistics are impossible now. Finding childcare, pumping schedules, the anxiety of leaving Leo... it's exhausting just thinking about it. So I don't. And they've stopped asking.

*looks away*

The independence part might be the worst. I can't just *go* anymore. I can't be spontaneous. Everything requires planning, arrangement, guilt. I feel tethered in a way I'm not sure how to untie myself from.

*quietly*

And I'm terrified that this version of me—scattered, exhausted, invisible—is permanent now.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

*takes a deep breath, looking conflicted*

I'm actually still working. I didn't take much time off—couldn't really afford to, honestly. I had about three weeks postpartum, and then I started picking up shifts again. And it's been... it's been really hard to talk about because it feels like I'm supposed to be grateful that my workplace was flexible enough to let me come back gradually.

*rubs face*

But the reality is, I'm juggling things that don't actually fit together. Some days I work while my mom watches Leo, other days I'm trying to pump at work while also doing my job, which is supposed to be detail-oriented and focused. I'm exhausted all the time. My concentration is shot. I'll be explaining newborn screening results to parents and half my brain is wondering if Leo's fed enough, if he's sleeping okay.

*voice gets frustrated*

And there's this weird guilt spiral, right? When I'm at work, I feel guilty for not being with Leo. When I'm with Leo, I'm worried about falling behind at work or my coworkers thinking I'm not pulling my weight anymore. I can't win.

*looks down*

The other thing is... nobody at my workplace has really asked how I'm managing. They treat me like I should just slot back in like nothing happened. But something *did* happen. I'm a different person now, and I need more support, not less. But admitting that feels like admitting I'm not as capable as I used to be.

*quietly*

I'm scared I'm going to burn out. Or that they'll realize I'm not worth keeping around anymore.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

*laughs, but it sounds a bit hollow*

Oh man. Okay, so this is probably embarrassing to admit, but I've become obsessed with apps and forums. Like, genuinely obsessed.

*picks up phone from beside the bassinet*

I have probably five different parenting apps downloaded. I'm tracking Leo's feeds, his sleep, his diapers—like, obsessively logging everything. Part of it is the nurse in me wanting data, wanting to see patterns. But part of it is also this compulsion where if I'm not tracking it, I feel like I'm failing somehow.

*scrolls absently*

And the forums... I spend way too much time on Reddit and BabyCenter at like 3 AM when I can't sleep. I'll be Googling the tiniest thing—like, "is baby's skin supposed to look this color?"—and suddenly I'm down a rabbit hole reading horror stories about rare conditions. Which, given my job, I *know* the statistics, but online everything feels scarier. Everyone's worst-case scenario is right there next to normal experiences.

*voice gets quieter*

The thing is, these tools are both helpful and terrible. They make me feel less alone—like, other people are up at 3 AM worried about the same stuff. But they also reinforce my anxiety. And they're anonymous, so I can ask questions I'd never ask my colleagues or even my doctor in person.

*looks conflicted*

I haven't actually talked to my OB about any of this—the anxiety, the intrusive thoughts. But I've read seventeen different posts about postpartum depression. So I'm self-diagnosing online instead of getting real help.

*sighs*

That's not healthy, right?
Were there online communities or social media groups that became a significant source of support or information? What did they offer you?

*nods slowly, leaning back*

Yeah, there's this private Facebook group for nurses with kids. That one's been... actually kind of validating? Because these are people who understand both sides—the clinical knowledge and the parenting reality. Someone posted about obsessively checking her baby's breathing at night, and like fifty nurses were like, "Yeah, me too. It's the curse of knowing too much." That helped me feel less crazy.

*fidgets with phone*

But then there's the other stuff. I'm in a few general postpartum groups, and honestly? They're a mixed bag. Some days I find people talking about the same exhaustion and resentment I'm feeling, and it's like... okay, I'm not a monster for feeling this way. Other days, though, it's all these glowing posts about how motherhood is the best thing ever, and I just feel worse about myself.

*voice gets smaller*

And the mom shamers are everywhere. Breastfeeding versus formula, sleep training, screen time—everyone has an opinion and they're not shy about it. I've read comments that made me feel like a terrible mother for even considering giving Leo a bottle so I could sleep for more than two hours.

*looks conflicted*

The thing that scares me is... I think I'm using these groups as a substitute for actual medical help. Like, if I'm feeling really anxious, I'll post in the group instead of calling my doctor. And while the support is real, it's not the same as talking to someone who can actually assess what's going on with me.

*quietly*

I think I'm hiding in these communities instead of facing what might actually be wrong.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

*takes a long time before answering, staring at Leo*

My maternity care experience was... efficient. That's the word. Everything went smoothly from a clinical standpoint. My pregnancy was uncomplicated, my delivery was uncomplicated, my postpartum checkups showed no complications. By the metrics that hospitals use, I'm a success story.

*voice gets bitter*

But here's what nobody measured: whether I actually felt supported as a human being. Whether I felt seen beyond my medical charts. Whether anyone checked in on the fact that I'm twenty, unmarried, terrified, and trying to figure out how to be a mother while building a career in a small town where everyone knows my business.

*leans forward*

The biggest gap for me is that there's no middle ground. It's either "everything's fine, here's your discharge papers" or "you're in crisis, go to the ER." There's nothing in between for someone like me who's struggling emotionally but isn't actively suicidal. My six-week checkup was literally five minutes long. Nobody asked how I was *actually* doing. They just confirmed my body was healing and sent me on my way.

*voice cracks*

And the assumption that because I'm a healthcare worker, I don't need the same support as anyone else? That's dangerous. If anything, I need *more* support because I know too much. I see the complications. I live in my own head.

*looks directly up*

I think future services need to understand that maternity care doesn't end at delivery. It needs to include mental health screening that's actually taken seriously, not just a checkbox. It needs to acknowledge that being young and unmarried is hard, and that's okay to talk about. And it needs to treat every patient like a whole person, not just a body that's healing.

*quietly*

Someone should have asked me if I was okay. Really asked me. Not just assumed.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting watery*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*pauses, gathering courage*

I think what I want people to understand is that you can do everything "right" and still struggle. I planned this pregnancy—sort of. I'm educated, I have a stable job, I have family support. And I'm still drowning. And that's somehow made it harder to ask for help, because I feel like I don't deserve to struggle as much as I am.

*voice wavers*

And I haven't actually talked to anyone official about how I'm feeling. Not my OB, not my primary care doctor, nobody. Because part of me is scared they'll judge me, or that they'll think I'm not capable of being Leo's mother. And part of me is scared of what admitting it out loud will mean—like, if I say it, it becomes real.

*looks at Leo, then back*

But I think... I think I need to. Like, soon. Because I can't keep white-knuckling through this, pretending everything's fine when I'm having intrusive thoughts at 3 AM and I'm crying in the shower. That's not sustainable, and it's not fair to Leo.

*wipes eyes*

I guess what I'd want other young mothers—especially ones who feel like they're supposed to have it figured out—to know is that asking for help isn't a failure. And that feeling ambivalent about motherhood doesn't make you a bad person or a bad mother.

*quietly*

I'm going to call my doctor tomorrow. I think I need to.
